deck: Liz Wiseman, author of the bestseller Multipliers, joins Amie Vaccaro and Jonathan Jackson to unpack the leadership research behind getting twice as much from your team, and why mission-driven organizations are especially prone to accidentally diminishing the people they lead.
seo_description: Liz Wiseman, author of Multipliers, on multiplier leadership, the accidental diminisher, and getting twice as much from your team in global health and development.
og_description: Liz Wiseman on multiplier leadership and why mission-driven teams are most prone to accidentally diminishing their people.
about: Jonathan Jackson and Amie Vaccaro sit down with Liz Wiseman, researcher, executive advisor, and author of the New York Times bestseller Multipliers, to explore what she calls multiplier leadership. Drawing on research that began with her years at Oracle, Liz explains the difference between diminishers, who run a one big brain model and access only about half of their people's intelligence, and multipliers, who invite and amplify the intelligence of those around them.

Much of the conversation centers on the accidental diminisher: the well-meaning, hyperactive leader whose strengths, like optimism, responsiveness, and a need for speed, end up crowding out the contributions of their team. Liz offers practical ways through this, from the player coach lesson she draws from Magic Johnson to sizing challenges smaller for newcomers and naming the difference between freeways and playgrounds where mistakes are safe.

The hosts press Liz on how this applies in resource-constrained global health and development work, where leaders are often both managing and contributing as individuals. Her research finds the diminishing dynamic can be even stronger in sacred and mission-driven organizations, where noble intent justifies hyperactive leadership. Her counterintuitive remedy: make your accidental diminisher tendencies a public, even laughable, conversation so the whole team helps keep them in check.

pull_quote.text: Most diminishing is coming from what I call the accidental diminisher. The person who really wants to be a good boss, who wants to use people's strengths, but yet even with the best of intentions and with seemingly good leadership strategies are having a diminishing effect.

bullets: Diminishers vs. multipliers: why the one big brain model gets half of people's intelligence, while multipliers get twice as much
The accidental diminisher: how strengths like optimism, responsiveness, and high standards quietly shut down the people around you
The player coach problem: Liz's Magic Johnson story on how to play big in a way that lets everyone else play big too
Multiplying newcomers: smaller two-week bites instead of two-year projects, and naming where it's safe to experiment
Why speed diminishes: the do-it-yourself tendency, and the upfront investment in defining what good looks like that pays time rebates later
The mission-driven trap: why diminishing runs strongest in nonprofits, schools, and churches where noble intent excuses hyperactive leadership
A culture fix: making your diminisher tendencies public so your whole team helps keep them in check

stats.label: more capability multiplier leaders draw out compared to diminishers, who get about half
the diminishing effect in high power distance cultures with strong deference to authority
Liz Wiseman spent at Oracle, where her leadership research began

guests.name: Liz Wiseman
guests.role: Researcher, executive advisor, and New York Times bestselling author of Multipliers, The Multiplier Effect, Impact Players, and Rookie Smarts.
